column33: Adult-onset autosomal dominant demyelinating leukodystrophy (ADLD) is a rare@ inherited neurological disorder that primarily affects the white matter of the brain. The condition typically begins in the fourth or fifth decade of life and is characterized by a range of neurological symptoms.
column34: Symptoms of ADLD typically involve motor@ autonomic@ and cognitive functions. Motor symptoms may include weakness@ stiffness@ and spasticity in the limbs@ leading to difficulties with walking and coordination. Autonomic dysfunction can manifest as problems with bowel and bladder control@ orthostatic hypotension (a drop in blood pressure upon standing)@ and abnormal sweating. Cognitive decline may also occur@ leading to memory loss and difficulties with executive functions. Additional symptoms can include vision problems@ swallowing difficulties@ and speech impairments.
column35: ADLD is caused by a genetic mutation that results in the duplication of the LMNB1 gene on chromosome 5q31.1. This genetic abnormality leads to an increased production of lamin B1@ a protein that is a component of the nuclear lamina@ which provides structural support to the cell nucleus. The overproduction of lamin B1 disrupts the normal structure and function of oligodendrocytes@ the cells responsible for producing myelin@ which insulates nerve fibers in the brain. The resulting demyelination leads to the neurological symptoms associated with ADLD.

These may include developmental delays@ intellectual disability@ seizures@ spasticity (muscle stiffness)@ dystonia (involuntary muscle movements)@ and microcephaly (abnormally small head size). Some individuals also experience skin lesions resembling those seen in autoimmune disorders.
column35: AGS is caused by mutations in genes involved in the type I interferon pathway@ which plays a crucial role in the immune system's response to viral infections. These genetic mutations lead to an overactivation of the interferon pathway@ causing inflammation and damage to the brain and other tissues.
column41: There is no specific cure for Aicardi-Goutières syndrome. Treatment focuses on managing the symptoms and providing supportive care.
column42: This may involve medications to control seizures@ manage spasticity@ and address other neurological issues. Physical therapy and occupational therapy can help improve motor skills and functional abilities.
column43: Nutritional support and specialized care may be necessary to address feeding difficulties and other medical complications.
column44: There is no known way to prevent Aicardi-Goutières syndrome@ as it is a genetic disorder caused by inherited mutations.
column45: Genetic counseling is recommended for families with a history of AGS to assess the risk of recurrence and provide information about available testing options.

column52: Diagnosis of Aicardi-Goutières syndrome typically involves a combination of clinical evaluation@ neuroimaging studies (such as MRI)@ and genetic testing to identify mutations in the associated genes.
column53: Complications of Aicardi-Goutières syndrome can include severe neurological dysfunction@ developmental delays@ intellectual disability@ seizures@ spasticity@ dystonia@ and other medical issues.
column54: Aicardi-Goutières syndrome is a rare disorder@ with an estimated prevalence of less than 1 in 100@000 individuals.
column55: The prognosis for individuals with Aicardi-Goutières syndrome varies depending on the severity of the condition and the extent of neurological involvement. Some individuals may have a shortened lifespan@ while others may live into adulthood with appropriate medical care and support.
